annotations.label: Context-free grammar

anchor.snippet: a context-free grammar ( CFG ) is a formal grammar whose production rules can be applied to a nonterminal symbol regardless of its context

annotations.note: ContextFreeGrammar is a structure with nonterminal type, initial symbol, and rules each rewriting a single nonterminal, matching the CFG definition.

annotations.label: Formal grammar

anchor.snippet: A formal grammar is essentially a set of production rules that describe all possible strings in a given formal language.

annotations.note: Mathlib only defines context-free grammars, not a general formal-grammar (arbitrary production system) structure.

annotations.label: Language generated by a grammar

anchor.snippet: The language generated by a grammar is the set of all strings of terminal symbols that can be derived

annotations.note: ContextFreeGrammar.language is the set of terminal words derivable from the initial nonterminal.

annotations.label: Context-free language

anchor.snippet: Languages generated by context-free grammars are known as context-free languages (CFL).

annotations.note: Language.IsContextFree L holds iff some context-free grammar generates L.

annotations.label: Language equality undecidable

anchor.snippet: do two given context-free grammars generate the same language?) is undecidable

annotations.note: No undecidability result about CFG language equivalence exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: CFG as 4-tuple

anchor.snippet: A context-free grammar G is defined by the 4- tuple

annotations.note: ContextFreeGrammar bundles terminals (type T), nonterminals (NT), rules, and the initial symbol, capturing the (V,Σ,R,S) tuple.

annotations.label: Nonterminal / variable

anchor.snippet: is called a nonterminal character or a variable

annotations.note: Symbol.nonterminal injects the nonterminal type into the grammar's symbol alphabet.

annotations.label: Terminals

anchor.snippet: is a finite set of terminal s, disjoint from

annotations.note: Symbol.terminal injects terminals, kept disjoint from nonterminals by the inductive Symbol type.

annotations.label: Production rules

anchor.snippet: The members of R are called the (rewrite) rule s or production s of the grammar

annotations.note: ContextFreeRule represents a production with an input nonterminal and an output string, collected in g.rules.

annotations.label: Start variable

anchor.snippet: S is the start variable (or start symbol), used to represent the whole sentence (or program).

annotations.note: The initial field of ContextFreeGrammar is the start nonterminal.

annotations.label: Production rule as pair

anchor.snippet: A production rule in R is formalized mathematically as a pair

annotations.note: ContextFreeRule is exactly a pair of an input nonterminal and an output string of symbols.

annotations.label: ε-production

anchor.snippet: is called an ε-production

annotations.note: An ε-production is expressible as a ContextFreeRule with empty output, but Mathlib has no named ε-production concept.

annotations.label: Alternatives

anchor.snippet: are called the first and second alternative, respectively.

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of bundled alternatives; each alternative is just a separate rule.

annotations.label: Directly yields

anchor.snippet: we say u directly yields v

annotations.note: ContextFreeGrammar.Produces u v is the one-step derivation relation via some rule of the grammar.

annotations.label: Yields / derived

anchor.snippet: we say u yields v or v is derived from u if there is a positive integer k

annotations.note: ContextFreeGrammar.Derives is the reflexive-transitive closure of Produces, i.e. the multi-step derivation relation.

annotations.label: Language of a grammar

anchor.snippet: of all terminal-symbol strings derivable from the start symbol.

annotations.note: ContextFreeGrammar.language is exactly the set of terminal words derivable from the initial symbol.

annotations.label: Context-free language (CFL)

anchor.snippet: A language L is said to be a context-free language (CFL), if there exists a CFG G

annotations.note: Language.IsContextFree L is defined as the existence of a CFG whose language equals L.

annotations.label: Pushdown automata recognize CFLs

anchor.snippet: Non-deterministic pushdown automata recognize exactly the context-free languages.

annotations.note: Mathlib has no pushdown automaton definition and hence no equivalence with context-free languages.

annotations.label: Words with reverse grammar

anchor.snippet: is context-free. It is not proper since it includes an ε -production.

annotations.note: This specific grammar/language example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Palindrome grammar

anchor.snippet: a context-free grammar for the set of all palindromes over the alphabet {a, b} is obtained.

annotations.note: No palindrome context-free grammar example is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Well-formed parentheses

anchor.snippet: The canonical example of a context-free grammar is parenthesis matching, which is representative of the general case.

annotations.note: Mathlib's DyckWord formalizes the balanced-parenthesis language directly, but not via a context-free grammar.

annotations.label: Nested parentheses and brackets

anchor.section: Well-formed nested parentheses and square brackets
anchor.snippet: A second canonical example is two different kinds of matching nested parentheses, described by the productions:

annotations.note: No two-type nested-bracket grammar example exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Matching pairs a^n b^n (n≥1)

anchor.snippet: In a context-free grammar, we can pair up characters the way we do with brackets .

annotations.note: The a^n b^n grammar example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Matching pairs a^n b^n (n≥0)

anchor.snippet: we obtain a grammar generating the language {a n b n : n ≥ 0} instead.

annotations.note: The a^n b^n (n≥0) grammar example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Unequal number of as and bs

anchor.section: Distinct number of as and bs
anchor.snippet: A context-free grammar for the language consisting of all strings over {a, b} containing an unequal number of a s and b s:

annotations.note: This unequal-count grammar example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Second block of bs double size

anchor.section: Second block of bs of double size
anchor.snippet: It is context-free as it can be generated by the following context-free grammar:

annotations.note: This specific grammar example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: First-order logic formulas

anchor.snippet: The formation rules for the terms and formulas of formal logic fit the definition of context-free grammar

annotations.note: Mathlib defines first-order formulas (ModelTheory) inductively but not as a context-free grammar instance.

annotations.label: Two-type balanced parentheses not CF

anchor.section: Examples of languages that are not context-free
anchor.snippet: there is no context-free grammar for generating all sequences of two different types of parentheses

annotations.note: No non-context-freeness result (e.g. pumping lemma application) is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regular grammars are context-free

anchor.snippet: Every regular grammar is context-free, but not all context-free grammars are regular.

annotations.note: Mathlib has no regular-grammar definition, so the inclusion into context-free grammars is not stated.

annotations.label: Regular context-free grammar

anchor.snippet: The following context-free grammar, for example, is also regular.

annotations.note: This regular-grammar example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regular grammar to NFA

anchor.snippet: Every regular grammar corresponds directly to a nondeterministic finite automaton

annotations.note: Mathlib has NFA but no regular-grammar definition or correspondence to it.

annotations.label: Derivation

anchor.snippet: A derivation of a string for a grammar is a sequence of grammar rule applications that transform the start symbol into the string.

annotations.note: ContextFreeGrammar.Derives (reflexive-transitive closure of Produces) captures derivation as a sequence of rule applications.

annotations.label: Leftmost / rightmost derivation

anchor.snippet: in a leftmost derivation , it is always the leftmost nonterminal; in a rightmost derivation , it is always the rightmost nonterminal.

annotations.note: Mathlib's derivation relation does not distinguish leftmost/rightmost rewriting.

annotations.label: Parse tree

anchor.snippet: This tree is called a parse tree or "concrete syntax tree" of the string

annotations.note: No parse tree / concrete syntax tree of a derivation is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ambiguous grammar

anchor.snippet: then the grammar is said to be an ambiguous grammar .

annotations.note: Grammar ambiguity is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inherently ambiguous language

anchor.snippet: such languages are called inherently ambiguous languages .

annotations.note: Inherent ambiguity of languages is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equivalent normal forms exist

anchor.snippet: Every context-free grammar with no ε -production has an equivalent grammar in Chomsky normal form , and a grammar in Greibach normal form .

annotations.note: Neither Chomsky nor Greibach normal form (nor their existence theorems) is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: CYK polynomial-time decision

anchor.snippet: one can use the Chomsky normal form to construct a polynomial-time algorithm that decides whether a given string is in the language

annotations.note: The CYK algorithm and its complexity are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Closure under operations

anchor.snippet: Context-free languages are closed under the various operations

annotations.note: Mathlib proves closure under reversal only; union, concatenation, and Kleene-star closure for CFLs are not formalized.

annotations.label: Non-closure under intersection

anchor.snippet: They are not closed under general intersection (hence neither under complementation ) and set difference.

annotations.note: Non-closure of CFLs under intersection/complement is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parsing is decidable

anchor.snippet: The parsing problem, checking whether a given word belongs to the language given by a context-free grammar, is decidable

annotations.note: No decidability instance for membership in a CFG's language is provided in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Valiant: parsing via matrix multiplication

anchor.snippet: Context-free parsing for Chomsky normal form grammars was shown by Leslie G. Valiant to be reducible to Boolean matrix multiplication

annotations.note: Valiant's reduction of CFG parsing to matrix multiplication is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lee: lower bound for CFG parsing

anchor.snippet: Conversely, Lillian Lee has shown

annotations.note: Lee's lower bound for CFG parsing is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Productive symbol

anchor.snippet: is called productive , or generating , if there is a derivation

annotations.note: The notion of a productive/generating symbol is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reachable symbol

anchor.snippet: is called reachable if there is a derivation

annotations.note: Reachability of grammar symbols is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Useless symbol

anchor.snippet: is called useless if it is unreachable or unproductive.

annotations.note: The notion of a useless symbol is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nullable symbol

anchor.snippet: is called nullable if there is a derivation

annotations.note: Nullability of grammar symbols is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cycle

anchor.snippet: is called a cycle .

annotations.note: The cycle notion for grammar derivations is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Elimination algorithms

anchor.snippet: Algorithms are known to eliminate from a given grammar, without changing its generated language,

annotations.note: Grammar-cleanup elimination algorithms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Proper grammar

anchor.snippet: A context-free grammar is said to be proper if it has neither useless symbols nor

annotations.note: The notion of a proper grammar is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Transform to proper grammar

anchor.snippet: every context-free grammar not generating ε can be transformed into a weakly equivalent proper one.

annotations.note: Transformation to a weakly-equivalent proper grammar is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regularity / LL(k) checks decidable

anchor.snippet: It is decidable whether a given grammar is a regular grammar

annotations.note: No decidability results about a grammar being regular or LL(k) are in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Language regularity undecidable

anchor.snippet: Given a context-free grammar, it is not decidable whether its language is regular

annotations.note: Undecidability of whether a CFL is regular is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Emptiness and finiteness decidable

anchor.snippet: There are algorithms to decide whether the language of a given context-free grammar is empty, as well as whether it is finite.

annotations.note: Decidability of emptiness/finiteness of a CFG's language is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Emptiness decidable for CFGs

anchor.snippet: is undecidable for context-sensitive grammars , but decidable for context-free grammars.

annotations.note: The decidability of CFG emptiness (vs. context-sensitive) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Universality undecidable

anchor.snippet: Given a CFG, does it generate the language of all strings over the alphabet of terminal symbols used in its rules?

annotations.note: Undecidability of CFG universality is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Language equality undecidable

anchor.snippet: Given two CFGs, do they generate the same language?

annotations.note: Undecidability of CFG language equality is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Language inclusion undecidable

anchor.snippet: Given two CFGs, can the first one generate all strings that the second one can generate?

annotations.note: Undecidability of CFG language inclusion is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chomsky hierarchy level undecidable

anchor.section: Being in a lower or higher level of the Chomsky hierarchy
anchor.snippet: Using Greibach's theorem , it can be shown that the two following problems are undecidable

annotations.note: Greibach's theorem and related undecidability results are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Grammar ambiguity undecidable

anchor.snippet: Given a CFG, is it ambiguous ?

annotations.note: Undecidability of grammar ambiguity is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Language disjointness undecidable

anchor.snippet: Given two CFGs, is there any string derivable from both grammars?

annotations.note: Undecidability of CFG language disjointness is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extended context-free grammar

anchor.snippet: An extended context-free grammar (or regular right part grammar ) is one in which the right-hand side of the production rules is allowed to be a regular expression

annotations.note: Extended (regular-right-part) context-free grammars are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extended CFGs describe CFLs

anchor.snippet: Extended context-free grammars describe exactly the context-free languages.

annotations.note: The equivalence of extended CFGs with CFLs is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Context-sensitive grammars

anchor.snippet: This produces the formalism of context-sensitive grammars .

annotations.note: Context-sensitive grammars are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: LR(k) grammars

anchor.snippet: LR( k ) grammars (also known as deterministic context-free grammars ) allow parsing (string recognition) with deterministic pushdown automata

annotations.note: LR(k)/deterministic context-free grammars are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: LL(k) grammars

anchor.snippet: LL( k ) and LL( * ) grammars allow parsing by direct construction of a leftmost derivation as described above

annotations.note: LL(k)/LL(*) grammars are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simple grammars

anchor.snippet: Simple grammars are a subclass of the LL(1) grammars mostly interesting for its theoretical property

annotations.note: Simple (s-) grammars are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bracketed grammars

anchor.snippet: Bracketed grammars have the property that the terminal symbols are divided into left and right bracket pairs that always match up in rules.

annotations.note: Bracketed grammars are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear grammars

anchor.snippet: Linear grammars have no rules with more than one nonterminal on the right-hand side.

annotations.note: Linear grammars are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regular grammars subclass

anchor.snippet: Regular grammars are a subclass of the linear grammars and describe the regular languages

annotations.note: Regular grammars (as a grammar subclass) are not defined in Mathlib.
